diaphragm-to-adt.title: From Diaphragm to Positive ADT
diaphragm-to-adt.start: Left hemidiaphragm loses ZOA (no liver support)
steps: Left diaphragm flattens, becomes postural instead of respiratory
Left psoas and iliacus shorten (share anterior lumbar attachments with crura)
Left innominate pulled into anterior tilt (IP ER: flexion, abduction, ER)
Left acetabulum rotates forward over the femur (AF ER)
Femoral head position changes relative to acetabular rim
In sidelying, femur cannot adduct past midline (impingement on acetabular rim)
Positive left ADT

outlet-to-padt.title: From Outlet Closure to Positive PADT
outlet-to-padt.start: Left outlet in IsP IR (closed, pelvic floor descended)
steps: Left ischial tuberosities are adducted (outlet closed)
Left pelvic floor is descended and cannot ascend
Left inlet is in IP ER (open) — ilium can't adduct
Distal iliacus is pulling femur, overriding proximal iliacus inlet adduction
In sidelying, patient cannot lower top knee to mat
Positive left PADT

left-aic-to-right-compensation.title: From Left AIC to Right Compensatory Position
left-aic-to-right-compensation.start: Left innominate in anterior tilt (L IP ER)
left-aic-to-right-compensation.end: Right innominate in compensatory position (R IP IR)
steps: Left pelvis rotates forward, sacrum rotates rightward
Spine orients to the right (follows the sacrum)
Body weight shifts to the right leg
Right innominate is loaded in weight-bearing and held posteriorly
Right inlet closes (R IP IR: extension, adduction, IR)
Right outlet opens (R IsP ER: flexion, abduction, ER)

iota-weakness-to-respiratory.title: From IO/TA Weakness to Respiratory Dysfunction
steps: Left abdominal wall loses tension
Left respiratory diaphragm loses ZOA (no abdominal counter-pressure)
Left anterior ribs flare into external rotation
Left anterior thorax hyperinflates (air goes where resistance is lowest)
Left posterior mediastinum can't expand (air is already anterior)
Right anterior upper chest can't expand (limited by left-dominant inflation pattern)
Positive right Apical Expansion Test

bpec-to-pubalgia.title: From B PEC to Pubalgia
bpec-to-pubalgia.start: Bilateral PEC pattern (both inlets in IP ER)
steps: Both inlets are in IP ER (bilateral anterior tilt)
Both outlets are in IsP IR (bilateral adduction/closure)
Rectus abdominis is eccentrically lengthened by bilateral anterior tilt
Paraspinals are short and tight (pulling spine into extension)
Adductors are lengthened by outlet IsP IR position
Opposing forces create shearing at pubic symphysis
Pubalgia

repositioning-to-restoration.title: From Repositioning to Pelvis Restoration Decision
repositioning-to-restoration.start: Patient presents with + bilateral ADT, + bilateral PADT
steps: Perform repositioning: 3 techniques matched to pattern
Retest ADT: now negative bilaterally (bone repositioned)
Retest PADT: still positive bilaterally (pelvic floor still dysfunctional)
The bone is in the right place but the pelvic floor can't cycle
This is a motor control deficit, not a positional problem
Enter Pelvis Restoration program

gameScenarios.explanation: Positive ADT and PADT on the left only (asymmetric) = Left AIC. Positive PART on the right is expected (lateral buttressing of right femoral head). SRT > 0 and Squat < 3/5 confirm not Patho.

repositioning.question: Which 3 repositioning techniques for Left AIC?
options: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correct: Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
repositioning.explanation: Left AIC uses asymmetric repositioning. The B PEC/Patho set (Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 in Passive FA IR with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift) is bilateral.

postReposition.question: Post-repositioning: − L ADT, − R ADT, + L PADT, + R PART. What program?
options: Reevaluate technique or other PRI courses
postReposition.explanation: − L ADT / + L PADT ± R PART → Left AIC Pelvis Restoration. ADT cleared (bone repositioned) but PADT positive (pelvic floor can't ascend).

facilitation.question: First muscle group to facilitate in the L AIC Facilitation Hierarchy?
options: R anterior inlet (R rectus femoris + R sartorius)
facilitation.correct: R anterior inlet (R rectus femoris + R sartorius)
facilitation.explanation: Step 1: Turn ON R anterior inlet (IP ER) via R rectus femoris + R sartorius. R HALT 0/5.

gameScenarios.explanation: Bilateral positive ADT/PADT (symmetric) with negative PART bilaterally = B PEC. SRT > 0 and Squat < 3/5 confirm standard B PEC, not Patho. Key: PART negative bilaterally.

repositioning.question: Which 3 repositioning techniques for Bilateral PEC?
options: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correct: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
repositioning.explanation: B PEC and B Patho PEC use the same three bilateral repositioning techniques.

postReposition.question: Post-repositioning: − B ADT, + B PADT. What program?
options: Reevaluate technique or other PRI courses
postReposition.explanation: − B ADT / + B PADT → Pelvis Restoration. If both had cleared → Myokinematic Restoration & Postural Respiration.

facilitation.question: First priority in the B PEC hierarchy after repositioning?
options: Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet
facilitation.explanation: B PEC hierarchy step 1: maximize diaphragmatic respiratory function. Before inlet/outlet inhibition (step 2) and L AIC alternating program (step 3).

gameScenarios.explanation: Patho = Paradoxical performance. Bilateral + ADT/PADT (like B PEC) but three distinguishers: (1) PART positive (B PEC: negative), (2) SRT = 0 / touches toes (B PEC: can't), (3) Squat > 3/5 (B PEC: < 3/5). Better ROM = worse pathology.

repositioning.question: Which 3 repositioning techniques for Bilateral Patho PEC?
options: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correct: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
repositioning.explanation: Same three as B PEC. Treatment tracks diverge after repositioning, not at the repositioning step.

postReposition.question: Patient needs a balloon exercise but Squat is 4/5. What do you do?
options: Substitute a straw, kazoo, or party favor
postReposition.correct: Substitute a straw, kazoo, or party favor
postReposition.explanation: Functional Squat > 3/5 (Patho) → patient likely cannot handle a balloon. Use straw, kazoo, or party favor. Noted twice in the manual.

facilitation.question: First priority in the B Patho PEC hierarchy?
options: Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet
facilitation.explanation: Same as B PEC: step 1 is respiratory function. But for Patho, substitute balloon with straw/kazoo/party favor.

gameScenarios.explanation: Asymmetric: + L ADT, + L PADT, + R PART. SRT uses hip-shift version (L AIC). Squat < 3/5, HALT 0/5 = early-stage L AIC.

repositioning.question: Which 3 repositioning techniques?
options: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correct: Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back

postReposition.question: Post-repositioning: − L ADT, − R ADT, + L PADT, − R PART. What program?
options: Reevaluate technique or other PRI courses
postReposition.explanation: − L ADT / + L PADT → Left AIC Pelvis Restoration. The R PART clearing doesn't change the pathway.

facilitation.question: R HALT is 0/5. Per the facilitation hierarchy, what first?
options: R anterior inlet via R rectus femoris + R sartorius
L anterior outlet via L pelvic floor muscles
L posterior inlet via L iliacus + L gluteus medius
R posterior outlet via R gluteus maximus
facilitation.correct: R anterior inlet via R rectus femoris + R sartorius
facilitation.explanation: HALT 0/5 = facilitation step 1: Turn ON R anterior inlet (IP ER) via R rectus femoris + R sartorius.

gameScenarios.explanation: Bilateral + ADT/PADT. PART positive (unilateral — manual says 'bilateral or unilateral' for Patho). SRT = 0 (touches toes). Squat 3/5 is borderline but positive PART + touching toes clinches it. B PEC would have negative PART and can't touch toes.

repositioning.question: Which 3 repositioning techniques?
options: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correct: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
repositioning.explanation: B Patho PEC → same three as B PEC.

postReposition.question: Post-repositioning: − B ADT, − B PADT. What program?
options: Reevaluate technique or other PRI courses
postReposition.explanation: − B ADT / − B PADT → Myokinematic Restoration & Postural Respiration. Both cleared = positional problem resolved, pelvic floor functioning.

facilitation.question: Both ADT and PADT cleared with repositioning alone. What does this mean?
options: Pelvic floor dysfunction was secondary to bone position — repositioning restored function
The patient has a Right AIC pattern
facilitation.correct: Pelvic floor dysfunction was secondary to bone position — repositioning restored function
facilitation.explanation: When both clear with repositioning, the problem was positional. The patient moves to Myokinematic Restoration — they don't need Pelvis Restoration.

gameScenarios.explanation: Bilateral + ADT/PADT, negative PART bilaterally, SRT > 0, Squat < 3/5 = standard B PEC. If PART were positive, SRT were 0, or Squat > 3/5 → suspect Patho.

repositioning.question: 'Passive FA IR' in the 90-90 Hip Lift — what does this mean?
options: Pelvis internally rotates on a fixed femur
Femur is passively placed in internal rotation on a fixed pelvis
Patient actively internally rotates the femur
Acetabulum externally rotates on the femur
repositioning.correct: Femur is passively placed in internal rotation on a fixed pelvis
repositioning.explanation: FA = Femoral-Acetabular = femur is the moving bone. 'Passive' = clinician positions it. IR = internal rotation. Standard hip IR in an open-chain position.

postReposition.question: 'Promote inlet extension' means:
options: Move inlets from IP ER (flexion/open) into IP IR (extension/closed)
postReposition.correct: Move inlets from IP ER (flexion/open) into IP IR (extension/closed)
postReposition.explanation: In B PEC both inlets are in IP ER (flexion, abduction, ER = open). 'Inlet extension' = IP IR (extension, adduction, IR = closed). IO/TAs are the primary movers.

facilitation.question: B PEC hierarchy ends with 'Left AIC Alternating Program.' Why?
options: Behind every Bilateral PEC pattern is a Left AIC pattern
The left side is always weaker
B PEC only affects the left side
facilitation.correct: Behind every Bilateral PEC pattern is a Left AIC pattern
facilitation.explanation: The bilateral compensation started as a left-sided problem (structural asymmetry). After addressing the bilateral component, address the underlying L AIC.

gameScenarios.explanation: Asymmetric: + L ADT/PADT, − R ADT/PADT, + R PART. SRT with L hip shift. Classic L AIC. Right side already partly neutral.

repositioning.question: R ADT already negative. After L AIC repositioning, L ADT clears. + L PADT, + R PART, R HALT 1/5. What facilitation step?
options: Step 1: R anterior inlet (R rectus femoris + R sartorius)
Step 2: L anterior outlet (L pelvic floor via adductors)
Step 3: L posterior inlet (L iliacus + L gluteus medius)
Step 4: R posterior outlet (R glute max)
repositioning.correct: Step 2: L anterior outlet (L pelvic floor via adductors)
repositioning.explanation: R HALT 1/5 = facilitation step 2: Turn ON L anterior outlet (IsP ER) via L pelvic floor muscles (via L adductors). The HALT score tells you where you are.

postReposition.question: All tests negative but patient is only 80% better. First thing to address?
options: Reciprocal L inlet IP IR / outlet IsP ER with inhalation and mid-zone with exhalation
postReposition.correct: Reciprocal L inlet IP IR / outlet IsP ER with inhalation and mid-zone with exhalation
postReposition.explanation: Neutrality achieved but symptoms persist → first: reciprocal inlet/outlet cycling with respiration. PRI Kegel is step 2 (requires HALT 4/5). Hormonal/psychosocial come later.

facilitation.question: PRI Kegel prerequisites?
options: Any HALT score with negative PADT
facilitation.explanation: PRI Kegel requires HALT 4/5 and neutral tests. Performed in L inlet IP IR / outlet IsP ER position.

gameScenarios.explanation: Bilateral + ADT/PADT. Patho tells: (1) PART positive (unilateral on R), (2) SRT = 0 (touches toes), (3) Squat > 3/5. HALT < 3/5 is consistent with all patterns.

repositioning.question: ADT cleared bilaterally, PADT still + bilaterally, Squat 4/5. Manual says 90-90 Hip Lift with Balloon. Adjustment?
options: Substitute straw, kazoo, or party favor
repositioning.correct: Substitute straw, kazoo, or party favor
repositioning.explanation: Squat > 3/5 (Patho) → substitute balloon. Noted twice in manual.

postReposition.question: Pelvic floor in B Patho PEC vs. B PEC?
options: Patho: descended, bulged, high resting tone. PEC: descended, hypertonic, inflexible.
Patho: ascended and weak. PEC: descended and hypertonic.
postReposition.correct: Patho: descended, bulged, high resting tone. PEC: descended, hypertonic, inflexible.
postReposition.explanation: Both descended. B PEC: hypertonic/inflexible (stuck, still has tone). Patho: bulged outward under pressure, high resting tone (muscles failing to resist IAP).

facilitation.question: B Patho PEC hierarchy step 3?
facilitation.explanation: Both B PEC and B Patho PEC hierarchies end with L AIC Alternating Program — because behind every bilateral PEC is a Left AIC.

visits.question: What pattern is this?
visits.explanation: Bilateral + ADT/PADT, negative PART, SRT > 0, Squat < 3/5 = B PEC. Not Patho (PART is negative, can't touch toes, low squat).
treatmentOptions: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correctTreatment: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon

visits.question: ADT cleared. PADT still positive. What program?
visits.explanation: − B ADT / + B PADT → Pelvis Restoration. Focus: mediastinum expansion and abs with reciprocal repositioning.

visits.question: R PADT cleared but L PADT still positive. R PART is now positive. What does the R PART finding indicate?
options: Right outlet can't abduct and left outlet can't adduct
visits.correct: Right outlet can't abduct and left outlet can't adduct
visits.explanation: Now showing L AIC characteristics emerging from under the B PEC. Promote L inlet adduction via L proximal iliacus.

visits.question: PADT now negative bilaterally. R PART still positive. What to address?
options: Promote R inlet abduction via R posterior glute max
visits.correct: Promote R inlet abduction via R posterior glute max
visits.explanation: With neutral PADT bilaterally and isolated + R PART, facilitate R glute max to address remaining R outlet/inlet deficit.

visits.question: All tests negative, HALT 4/4. What to integrate next?
options: R outlet adduction via R inferior glute max
visits.correct: R outlet adduction via R inferior glute max
visits.explanation: HALT 4/4 = near-complete frontal plane control. Integrate R outlet adduction to reach HALT 5/5.

visits.question: HALT 5/5, Squat 4/5. What final phase?
options: Reciprocal alternating function (retro walking, heel stair descents, reverse squatting)
visits.correct: Reciprocal alternating function (retro walking, heel stair descents, reverse squatting)
visits.explanation: Full frontal plane control achieved. Final phase: gait integration with reciprocal alternating activities.

visits.question: What pattern?
visits.explanation: Asymmetric: + L ADT, + L PADT, + R PART. SRT > 0. Classic Left AIC.
treatmentOptions: 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back
correctTreatment: Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back

visits.question: ADT and PADT both negative bilaterally. R PART still positive. What to do?
options: Inhibit L posterior mediastinum, promote R inlet abduction via R glute max
visits.correct: Inhibit L posterior mediastinum, promote R inlet abduction via R glute max
visits.explanation: With neutral ADT/PADT and isolated + R PART: address remaining R posterior inlet deficit via R glute max. Also address respiratory component (L posterior mediastinum).

visits.question: All tests negative. What to integrate?
options: L IO/TAs with R outlet adduction
visits.correct: L IO/TAs with R outlet adduction
visits.explanation: All tests negative = neutral pelvis achieved. Integrate L IO/TAs (the conduit between respiratory and pelvic diaphragms) with R outlet adduction for full reciprocal function.

decisionTree.question: Perform ADT bilaterally. What are the results?

next.question: Perform PADT bilaterally. What are the results?
Check PART, SRT, and Squat to determine pattern.

branches.answer: − bilateral PART, SRT > 0, Squat < 3/5 → Bilateral PEC

next.question: Reposition with: (1) Standing Wall Supported Reach, (2) 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon, (3) Modified All Four Belly Lift. Retest ADT.

next.content: Myokinematic Restoration & Postural Respiration program. Both position (ADT) and function (PADT) resolved.
Pelvis Restoration program. B PEC Hierarchy: (1) Maximize respiratory function, (2) Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet / posterior outlet, (3) Left AIC Alternating Program.
Reevaluate technique. Consider other PRI courses or interdisciplinary referral.

branches.answer: + PART (bilateral or unilateral), SRT = 0, Squat > 3/5 → Bilateral Patho PEC

next.question: Reposition with SAME 3 techniques as B PEC. Note: if Squat > 3/5, use straw/kazoo/party favor instead of balloon. Retest ADT.

next.content: Myokinematic Restoration & Postural Respiration. Both resolved with repositioning alone.

Pelvis Restoration program. B Patho PEC Hierarchy: (1) Maximize respiratory function (straw/kazoo, NOT balloon), (2) Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet / posterior outlet, (3) Left AIC Alternating Program.
Reevaluate. Patho PEC is the most decompensated pattern — may need longer repositioning or manual techniques.

next.question: Confirm: + L PADT, + R PART. This is Left AIC. Reposition with: (1) 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, (2) R Sidelying Respiratory L Adductor Pull Back, (3) L Sidelying R Glute Max. Retest.

branches.answer: − L ADT, − L PADT, − R PART
− L ADT, + L PADT ± R PART

next.question: Left AIC Pelvis Restoration. Check R HALT to determine facilitation step.

next.content: Step 1: Turn ON R anterior inlet (IP ER) via R rectus femoris + R sartorius.
Step 2: Turn ON L anterior outlet (IsP ER) via L pelvic floor muscles (via L adductors). Pelvic diaphragm ascension.
Step 3: Turn ON L posterior inlet (IS ER) via L iliacus + L gluteus medius. Counter-nutation on left.
Step 5: Upright L single-leg frontal plane control (L outlet abduction via L iliacus, L obturator, L iliococcygeus).
Step 4: Turn ON R posterior outlet (SI IR) via R glute max (coccygeus, piriformis). Then progress to steps 5-10.
Reevaluate repositioning technique or consider other PRI courses.

byMuscle.pattern: Corrects L AIC (L inlet in IP ER → needs IP IR)
byMuscle.hierarchyStep: L AIC Facilitation steps 1-3, also PADT correction

byMuscle.action: Posteriorly tilts pelvis, connects respiratory diaphragm to pelvic inlet
byMuscle.pattern: Weak in L AIC (left side), weak bilaterally in B PEC / B Patho PEC
byMuscle.hierarchyStep: Integration muscle (Yellow family). Used throughout all hierarchies.

byMuscle.action: Flexes right inlet (opens right top ring = R IP ER)
byMuscle.pattern: Facilitates R anterior inlet to oppose L AIC
exercises: Supine Hemi Extension w/ Alternating Respiratory Rectus Femoris and Sartorius

byMuscle.action: Flexes right inlet (assists R rectus femoris)
byMuscle.pattern: Facilitates R anterior inlet to oppose L AIC
exercises: Supine Hemi Extension w/ Alternating Respiratory Rectus Femoris and Sartorius

byMuscle.action: Abducts left outlet (opens bottom ring), adducts femur in closed chain
byMuscle.pattern: Corrects L AIC (L outlet in IsP IR → needs IsP ER)
exercises: R Sidelying Respiratory L Adductor Pull Back

byMuscle.action: Abducts left outlet, ascends pelvic floor
byMuscle.pattern: Corrects L AIC (L pelvic diaphragm descended → needs ascension)
exercises: R Sidelying Respiratory L Adductor Pull Back

byMuscle.action: Controls left outlet, contributes to pelvic floor ascension
Controls left outlet, contributes to pelvic floor ascension
Counter-nutates left ilium on sacrum (IS ER), frontal plane pelvic stabilizer
byMuscle.pattern: Corrects L AIC (L posterior inlet in IS IR → needs IS ER)
exercises: L Sidelying R Glute Max (L glute med co-activates)

byMuscle.action: Nutates right ilium on sacrum (R IS IR)
byMuscle.pattern: Facilitates R posterior inlet position to oppose L AIC
exercises: Supine Hooklying Synchronized Resisted Glute Max
byMuscle.hierarchyStep: L AIC Facilitation step 4 / also R posterior inlet

byMuscle.action: Closes right posterior outlet (R SI IR), extends hip
exercises: Supine Hooklying Synchronized Resisted Glute Max

byMuscle.action: Stabilizes right sacrum, contributes to R posterior outlet closure
Controls coccyx position, contributes to R posterior outlet closure
Pulls ischium down/back, posteriorly tilts pelvis
exercises: 90-90 Hip Lift positions (hamstrings activate to maintain 90-90)

byFinding.meaning: Left innominate anteriorly rotated. Femur can't adduct.
byFinding.muscles: Not a muscle problem — positional. Requires repositioning.
exercises: L AIC Repositioning: 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, R Sidelying Respiratory L Adductor Pull Back, L Sidelying R Glute Max

byFinding.muscles: Not a muscle problem — positional. Requires repositioning.
exercises: B PEC Repositioning: Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift

byFinding.meaning: Left pelvic diaphragm can't ascend. Outlet closed, inlet open.
byFinding.muscles: Facilitate: L proximal iliacus (inlet adduction), L obturator internus + L iliococcygeus (outlet abduction), L pelvic floor (ascension)

byFinding.meaning: Bilateral pelvic diaphragm dysfunction. Both outlets closed, both inlets open.
byFinding.muscles: Facilitate: bilateral IO/TAs, bilateral proximal iliacus, bilateral pelvic floor
exercises: B PEC or B Patho PEC hierarchy: respiratory function first, then inlet/outlet inhibition, then L AIC alternating
byFinding.hierarchyStep: B PEC / B Patho PEC hierarchy steps 1-3

byFinding.meaning: Right outlet can't abduct / right inlet can't abduct. Right pelvic diaphragm can't descend.

byFinding.finding: SRT = 0 with + B ADT and + B PADT
byFinding.meaning: Patient can touch toes despite bilateral dysfunction = Bilateral Patho PEC (compensatory hypermobility, not flexibility)
byFinding.muscles: All inlet and outlet muscles are 'OFF' (mechanically disadvantaged). Everything needs facilitation.
exercises: B Patho PEC hierarchy. Use straw/kazoo instead of balloon.
byFinding.hierarchyStep: Repositioning first, then B Patho PEC hierarchy

byFinding.meaning: High squat score with positive ADT = Patho PEC. Compensatory strategy, not motor control.
byFinding.muscles: Global muscle insufficiency — do not progress aggressively
exercises: Respiratory exercises with straw/kazoo (not balloon). Small movements.

byFinding.meaning: Can't even position for the test. Malaligned pelvis.
byFinding.muscles: R rectus femoris + R sartorius (R anterior inlet)

byFinding.meaning: IO/TA weakness, can't inhibit inlet abduction of flexed leg.
byFinding.muscles: L pelvic floor (obturator internus, iliococcygeus) via L adductors
exercises: R Sidelying Respiratory L Adductor Pull Back

byFinding.meaning: Poor IC adductor / anterior glute med. Can't achieve outlet abduction of flexed leg.
Poor contralateral adductor / ipsilateral glute med integration.
byFinding.muscles: R glute max (inferior fibers), R piriformis, R coccygeus

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI reversed the standard joint name ('sacroiliac') to 'ilio-sacral' to encode that the ilium is the bone they consider displaced. The 'I' comes first because PRI treats the ilium as the mover.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Psoas and iliacus pulled the ilium forward relative to the sacrum. The sacrum stayed. The resulting relative angle at the SI joint is called nutation.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: IS = target the ilium. Facilitate muscles that pull the ilium back: L gluteus medius (counter-nutation). L AIC Facilitation step 3.

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same renaming as IS IR — 'ilio-sacral' to indicate the ilium is the bone of interest. ER = the ilium has rotated outward/back.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Gluteus medius and/or hamstrings pulled the ilium backward relative to the sacrum. The resulting position is counter-nutation.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: IS = target the ilium. This is the goal position for the L side in L AIC. Achieved by facilitating L gluteus medius.

translationMap.realStructure: Sacroiliac joint (same joint as IS IR/ER)
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI kept the standard name order ('sacro-ilio') to encode that the sacrum is the bone they consider displaced. Same joint as IS, different clinical frame. The 'S' comes first because PRI treats the sacrum as the mover here.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Gluteus maximus pulled the sacrum backward/inward relative to the ilium. The posterior outlet closes.
translationMap.standardTerm: No distinct standard term. In conventional anatomy this is still motion at the SI joint. PRI creates a separate name to distinguish 'treat the sacrum' from 'treat the ilium.'
translationMap.encodedTreatment: SI = target the sacrum. Facilitate R glute max (inferior fibers), R piriformis, R coccygeus. L AIC Facilitation step 4.

translationMap.realStructure: Sacroiliac joint (same joint as IS IR/ER)
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same as SI IR — sacrum-first naming to indicate sacrum is the target.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: The sacrum rotated outward relative to the ilium, possibly due to piriformis overactivity or loss of glute max control. The posterior outlet opens.
translationMap.standardTerm: No distinct standard term. Motion at the SI joint.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: SI = target the sacrum. Correct by facilitating R glute max into SI IR.

translationMap.realStructure: Pubic symphysis (where the two innominates meet anteriorly). Not a standard joint in the way the SI or hip joint is — the pubic symphysis is a cartilaginous joint with very little motion.
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI created a joint name ('ilio-pubo') that doesn't exist in standard anatomy. There is no 'ilio-pubo joint.' PRI is describing innominate bone motion relative to the pubic symphysis midline. 'External rotation' here means the innominate rotated outward/anteriorly relative to that midline.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Psoas, iliacus, and rectus femoris pulled the innominate into anterior tilt. This is a tri-planar event: forward (sagittal = flexion), outward (frontal = abduction), and rotated (transverse = external rotation). PRI describes all three planes simultaneously as 'IP ER.'
translationMap.standardTerm: No single standard term. The closest conventional description: 'anterior pelvic tilt with innominate anterior rotation.' But this only captures the sagittal plane. PRI's tri-planar descriptor (flex/abd/ER) is actually more complete than the standard term — this is one case where PRI adds information rather than losing it.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: IP = targets the ilio-pubo region (anterior inlet). Correct by inhibiting via IO/TAs and posterior pelvic tilt. Repositioning clears ADT first.

translationMap.realStructure: Pubic symphysis (same non-standard 'joint' as IP ER)
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same invented joint. IR = innominate rotated inward/posteriorly relative to midline.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: IO/TAs and proximal iliacus pulled the innominate into posterior tilt / adduction / internal rotation. The inlet closes.
translationMap.standardTerm: Closest: 'posterior pelvic tilt with innominate posterior rotation.' Again, only captures sagittal plane.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: This is the goal position for the L side. Achieved by facilitating L proximal iliacus + L IO/TA.

translationMap.realStructure: Inferior pubic rami / ischiopubic ramus. Not a standard joint — this is a bony junction (the ischium and pubis are fused in adults). PRI is describing the position of the lower pelvis relative to the pubic symphysis.
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI created another non-standard joint name ('ischio-pubo'). There is no ischio-pubo joint. PRI is describing the ischial tuberosity position relative to midline. 'Internal rotation' = the ischia are adducted (squeezed together), closing the outlet.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: The outlet closed because the inlet opened (they move in opposite directions on the same bone). When psoas/iliacus pulled the top of the innominate forward (IP ER), the bottom of the same bone (the ischium) was pulled inward. The pelvic floor descended under pressure with the outlet squeezed shut.
translationMap.standardTerm: No standard term. 'Pelvic floor descent with ischial adduction' is the closest description.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: IsP = targets the ischio-pubo region (anterior outlet). Correct by facilitating L pelvic floor muscles (obturator internus, iliococcygeus) to open the outlet. L AIC Facilitation step 2.

translationMap.realStructure: Inferior pubic rami / ischiopubic ramus (same as IsP IR)
translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same non-standard joint. ER = the ischia have abducted (spread apart), opening the outlet.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Pelvic floor muscles (obturator internus, iliococcygeus) contracted, pulling the ischial tuberosities laterally. The outlet opens. The pelvic floor ascends.
translationMap.standardTerm: No standard term. 'Pelvic floor ascension with ischial abduction.'
translationMap.encodedTreatment: Goal position for L side. Achieved via L adductor facilitation activating L pelvic floor.

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI reversed standard 'femoral-acetabular' to 'acetabular-femoral' to encode that the acetabulum (pelvis) is the moving bone. In standard kinesiology, hip motion is described as the femur moving on the pelvis (FA). PRI's AF frame describes the pelvis moving on the femur — a closed-chain perspective.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: In standing (closed chain), IO/TAs posteriorly tilted the pelvis, glute med stabilized it, hamstrings pulled the ischium down — the net effect rotated the pelvis back over the planted femur.
translationMap.standardTerm: Closest: 'closed-chain posterior pelvic tilt' or 'closed-chain hip internal rotation.' But these are rarely used as compound terms in standard kinesiology because conventional descriptions focus on the femur.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: AF = target the pelvis (over the femur). This is the goal position for the L side — the net result of the entire facilitation hierarchy. Final integration at step 10.

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same reversed naming. AF ER = the pelvis has rotated forward/outward over the femur.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: Psoas, iliacus, and rectus femoris pulled the pelvis into anterior tilt over a planted femur. This is the L AIC position viewed from the hip joint.
translationMap.standardTerm: Closest: 'closed-chain anterior pelvic tilt.' Equivalent to L IP ER viewed from a different frame.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: AF = target the pelvis. Correct by repositioning first (clear ADT), then full facilitation hierarchy.

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: PRI kept the standard naming order here — femur first, because the femur is the moving bone. This is the one PRI term that matches standard kinesiology exactly.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: The femur rotated inward on a relatively fixed pelvis. Standard open-chain hip internal rotation.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: FA = the femur is being moved. Not a pathological finding. Used in treatment positions: 'Passive FA IR' means the clinician passively places the femur in internal rotation.

translationMap.whatPriRenamed: Same as FA IR — standard naming, femur is the mover.
translationMap.whatActuallyHappened: The femur rotated outward on a relatively fixed pelvis. Standard open-chain hip external rotation.
translationMap.encodedTreatment: FA = the femur moved. Hyperactive FA ERs are implicated at Functional Squat level 2 failure. Inhibited by achieving femoral adduction and inlet extension.
